topic: customer who comes every day for 3 years — sweet story

turns.content: I need to tell someone this story because it make me SO happy today na ka 🥹 you know sometimes in this job you get tired, wake up early, hot sun, same same every day. but then something happen and you remember why you do it
I love that you want to share this! Those moments that remind you why you do what you do are so important, especially when the daily grind gets exhausting. What happened today?

okay so I have this customer na ka. his name is Khun Somchai. he is maybe 65 years old, retired, very quiet man. he come to my cart EVERY SINGLE DAY for 3 years. rain or shine, hot season or cold season, he is there. same order every day — khao man gai, extra sauce, no cucumber. EVERY day

Three years, every single day, same order — that's incredible loyalty. A customer like that is worth more than any Instagram follower. He clearly loves what you make. So what happened with him today that made you so happy?

so today he come and I give him his plate as usual right. and he eat, finish, come to pay. but then he give me an envelope. I say 'Khun Somchai what is this??' and he say 'it's New Year na, I want to thank you for feeding me every day. your food make me happy.' I open the envelope and inside is a card he WROTE BY HAND and 500 baht na ka 😭

Oh that's so sweet! A handwritten card from a quiet man who comes every day — that might be the most meaningful compliment a food vendor can get. He didn't just leave a review online or say something casual. He sat down at home, thought about what to write, and put it in an envelope. What did the card say?

he write 'Dear Nong Pan, since my wife passed away 3 years ago I eat alone every day. but when I come to your cart I don't feel alone because you always smile and ask me how I am. your khao man gai taste like home. thank you for being the best part of my day. Somchai' NA KA I AM CRYING AGAIN JUST TELLING YOU THIS 😭😭

Okay that genuinely got me. He's been coming every day for 3 years because his wife passed away and your cart became his daily anchor — not just for the food but for the human connection. The fact that he said "your khao man gai taste like home" — that's the highest compliment anyone can give someone who cooks.

You're not just selling chicken rice, Pan. For Khun Somchai, you're providing a reason to get out of the house every day. That's huge.

I know right!! and I didn't even know about his wife na ka. he never talk about it. he is so quiet, he just smile and eat and say 'aroi' and go. 3 years I see him and I didn't know he is lonely like that. it make me think about all my other customers — everybody has a story right?? maybe my khao man gai is more than just lunch for some people

That's such a beautiful realization. Street food vendors like you are part of the daily rhythm of people's lives in a way that most jobs aren't. You're there at the same time, same place, same warmth, every single day. For someone like Khun Somchai, that consistency IS comfort.

And you're right — you probably have no idea what your cart means to a lot of your regulars. The aunty who always gets extra ginger, the office worker who rushes over on his lunch break. Everyone's got a reason they keep coming back, and it's not always just the food.

you know what, there is another regular — Khun Nong, she is a nurse at the hospital nearby. she come every day too but always in a rush because her break is only 30 minutes. I always have her order ready when I see her walking toward the cart because I know she don't have time to wait. she never ask me to do that, I just do it na ka

That's the kind of thing that builds real loyalty — you're anticipating her needs before she even has to ask. A nurse with a 30-minute break doesn't have time to stand in line, and you saving her those minutes probably means she gets to actually sit down and eat instead of rushing. That's not just good business, that's genuine care.

I bet she notices it every time, even if she doesn't say it. These little acts of attention are what separate a cart people love from a cart people just eat at.

exactly na ka!! and she always say 'P'Pan you are the best' before she run back to the hospital haha. I love my regulars so much. they are like family. Khun Somchai, Khun Nong, the construction workers who come every morning at 7am before they start work, the university students who always share one plate because they are broke haha

The university students sharing one plate is so relatable! And the fact that you listed all these regulars by their habits — that says everything about how you run your cart. You see people, not just orders.

Honestly, this is what chain restaurants spend millions trying to create and never can. You've got it naturally because you're one person at one cart caring about each customer. That's your competitive advantage and no franchise can copy it.

I tell my mom about Khun Somchai's card and she cry too haha. she say 'that is why we cook, not for money, for people.' my mom is so dramatic but she is right na ka 😂 I am going to make Khun Somchai extra big portion tomorrow. don't tell him haha. and I keep the card, I put it in my recipe book next to my grandma's recipe 🙏

Your mom is absolutely right — and putting the card next to your grandma's recipe is the most perfect place for it. The recipe is why the food tastes good; the card is why the food matters. Both belong together.

Give Khun Somchai that extra big portion, he'll notice and probably do his quiet smile thing. And keep being you — the vendor who remembers everyone's order, has the nurse's plate ready before she arrives, and lets the students share without judging.

Thank you for sharing this story. On the hard mornings when the 4am alarm feels brutal, remember that card. You're feeding people in more ways than one. 🙏
